name: EU AI Act — High-Risk System
description: Systems listed in Annex III requiring mandatory conformity assessment, registration in the EU database, and post-market monitoring.
notes: Mandatory conformity assessment, registration in EU database, post-market monitoring. Applies to AI in critical infrastructure, education, employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration, justice.

name: EU AI Act — Prohibited Practice
description: Systems whose deployment is absolutely prohibited regardless of safeguards: social scoring, real-time biometric surveillance, manipulation of vulnerable groups.
notes: Absolute prohibition. No conformity assessment path exists. Includes: subliminal manipulation, exploitation of vulnerabilities, real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces, social scoring by public authorities.

name: Open-Source Model (e.g., Llama, Mistral)
description: Publicly released model weights with permissive or open licences, freely downloadable and redistributable.
notes: High transferability + high replicability + low access control = open-source profile. Value capture flows to downstream deployers, not model owners. Governance gap: open release forecloses post-deployment control.

name: Closed API Model (e.g., GPT, Claude)
description: Model weights are proprietary; access is gated through a commercial API with usage terms. Deployer captures near-total value.
notes: High value capture + low transferability + high access control = walled garden. Deployer retains full rents while disclaiming liability for downstream harms. This is the dominant commercial configuration globally.

name: Frontier Model — Safety Evaluation Stage
description: Exists; capabilities confirmed; not publicly available. Undergoing internal or third-party safety evaluation before any release decision.
notes: Maximum capability + maximum restriction. The safety testing paradox: you need to deploy to test, but deployment creates risk. No governance framework currently mandates pre-release evaluation before capabilities reach this level.

name: UK Pro-Innovation AI Regulation
description: Principles-based, sector-specific framework with no single AI regulator and no mandatory pre-deployment approval pathway.
authority: DSIT AI White Paper (2023); Bletchley Declaration (2023)
notes: Wide ranges = deliberately flexible. Five cross-sector principles (safety, transparency, fairness, accountability, contestability) applied by existing sector regulators. No mandatory registration. Contrast with EU's prescriptive approach.

name: PRC AI Governance (Algorithm / GenAI / Deep Synthesis)
description: Three overlapping regulations covering algorithmic recommendations, generative AI services, and synthetic media, all requiring security assessment before deployment.
authority: Algorithm Recommendation Regulation (2022); GenAI Measures (2023); Deep Synthesis Provisions (2023)
notes: Low autonomy ceiling reflects mandatory content control and socialist core values alignment. Pre-launch security assessment required for GenAI services with public-facing outputs. ICP licence required for online deployment.

name: Unregulated Deployment
description: No governance framework applies. All dimensions unconstrained. This is the current default for most AI deployments globally.
notes: All sliders unconstrained. No framework matches because no framework exists. This is the current default for most AI deployments globally — including most closed API models operating outside the EU.
